original_abstract: The dynamic restructuring of Copper (Cu) electrodes under electrochemical conditions is thought to be a fundamental driver of its unique catalytic activity toward the $CO_2$ reduction reaction ($CO_2$RR). Specifically, surface roughening—characterized by the formation of atomic vacancies and adatoms—is a critical activation phenomenon, yet the precise atomistic nature of these motifs under realistic adsorbate coverages remains poorly understood. In this work, we investigate the chemical space of Cu surface restructuring under mixed CO and H coverage using a combination of grand canonical density functional theory (GCDFT) and global optimization techniques. By sampling tens of thousands of unique states, we construct a potential-dependent grand canonical ensemble of the Cu(100), (111), and (711) interfaces. We observe significant vertical displacement of surface Cu atoms within a specific regime of intermediate CO and H coverage. Because this regime is kinetically controlled rather than thermodynamically favored, we developed a quasi-kinetic Monte Carlo simulation to track the non-equilibrium evolution of the electrode during a cathodic scan. Our simulations reveal that the system becomes trapped in metastable states featuring elevated Cu-CO species. Chemical bonding analysis indicates that these species are stabilized by the joint influence of atop CO ligands and surrounding H atoms, which "lubricate" the surface by weakening Cu-Cu metallic bonds. Furthermore, molecular dynamics and free energy calculations identify a novel wave-like propagation mechanism for Cu-CO diffusion and subsequent dimerization. These findings provide essential atomistic insights into the roughening and clustering of Cu catalysts, offering a methodological framework to study complex, non-equilibrium interfaces in electrocatalysis.
humanized_abstract: Surface Reconstruction and Adsorbate-Induced Structure Evolution at Copper Electrodes during CO2 Reduction The surface reconstruction of Copper (Cu) during the catalytic CO2 reduction reaction ($CO_2$RR) has been widely studied, attributing the unique performance of Cu catalysts towards $CO_2$RR. The activation process associated with the surface roughening and related Cu catalytic activity is attributed to atomic vacancies and adatoms due to Cu surface reconstruction. However, the atomistic structure of the reconstructed surface and the related Cu active sites that exist at the catalyst surface under different adsorbate coverages remains unclear. Here, the chemical space of Cu surface reconstruction at mixed CO and H adsorbates was investigated using grand canonical density functional theory (GCDFT) and global optimization methods. A grand canonical ensemble of Cu(100), Cu(111), and Cu(711) interfaces was constructed from thousands of unique states. These simulations demonstrated that there is a considerable vertical displacement of surface Cu atoms in the mixed CO and H coverage regime. This regime is kinetically controlled, and thus quasi-kinetic Monte Carlo (QKC) simulations were performed to understand the non-equilibrium structural evolution of the electrode during a cathodic scan. These simulations revealed that the Cu surface gets trapped in metastable states containing Cu-CO species. The adsorption of these Cu-CO species is due to the cooperative effects of the atop CO adsorbates and the surrounding H adsorbates that “lubricate” the Cu surface by increasing the Cu-Cu bond-breaking propensity. Furthermore, molecular dynamics and free energy simulations revealed a wave-like diffusion of Cu-CO species, which leads to the dimerization of Cu-CO species and further catalyzing the $CO_2$RR reaction. In summary, this study provides new insights into the atomistic structure evolution of Cu catalysts during $CO_2$RR and highlights the simulation strategy that can be applied to investigate the non-equilibrium interfaces and structures of other complex transition metal electrodes in the context of heterogeneous electrocatalysis.
